Hi, and welcome to the very first recorded live webinar for Habritology. We recorded this one in advance for you today so that you would have something actionable the minute you got in the door. Today I'm Christine Potter and I am sitting down with Mark McCrae, Bond and Kevin Halbert, and David Franklin, and we are your Habritology team. We are going to have a little back and forth today. I've got some questions prepared to ask all of these guys. And just to let you know, we've come together from all over the world to do this for you today. Mark's based in Australia, Bond and Kevin are in California, David's on the West Coast as well, and I'm in Ohio, so it really is possible for us to gather anyone anywhere in the world to bring you Habritology. So today we're going to start the process that Kevin just recently told me that he wants to see Habritology do. So let's get down to it and start picking people's brains. I'd like to start with Mark. And what I want to know is the thing that I got the most excited about in your PowerPoint presentation and your video that you did for Habritology last week about harmony versus balance. Can you explain what harmony is for successful business owners and how it's different from balance? Sure. So thanks for the question, Christine. And for me, the difference is feeling good about yourself and your life. And what people try to do when they have balance is that they allocate time or they try and put people in pigeon holes or they say that this is how your life should look. And they base it around an antiquated idea of people who work nine to five and have set routines and do different things. When really I believe that the world is no longer at a desktop, you know, your life is mobile now. And one of the things I think people get very stressed about is the fact that they are constantly feel like they're under pressure because they're living in other people's agendas. You know, they're trying to keep pace with people who live nine to five and maybe they are on the internet and they work night shift or they have an odd hours. And with myself, I have very odd hours depending on who I'm working with because like now I'm working with the West Coast and East Coast. So if I was a traditional person who had a balanced life, this would feel very bad and uncomfortable for me because I'm awake when I shouldn't be. But if you're in harmony with your life and people around you understand, they understand you and they understand how you work, you don't feel that pressure. And it's a lot more harmonious. It feels a lot less stressed. And I think you get a lot more done, you're more productive. So the harmony is really about everybody is different. Everybody is unique and how they live their life should be how they live their life. They shouldn't try to fit in with everybody else. So that's kind of my explanation of harmony and balance. Okay, I have a follow-up question. Okay. How does your family handle that? Well, I've been with my wife now over 20 years. And I've worked most of that night shift for one reason or another. And my family, I think they enjoy it because I'm always at home. I have an office that I never go to. So I think I've been maybe two or three times in three or four years. In fact, it's been so long since when I went there that receptionist didn't know who I was. But they like it. They just accept the fact that I'm always around and I'm always accessible. And there are certain things that we do try and do that, you know, as it again, it's with them, we try and get that idea of harmony because they have so many different activities and things that they do as a family. We work together. So what we try and do is we have regular meetings, usually dinner and we'll discuss what the week looks like and we'll discuss who's doing what and who's going to attend what and who's going to support each other. And right down to choosing, I have an eight year old and an 11 year old boy and a 13 year old girl. So we rotate who has a choice of which restaurant we're going to go to, for example, or who we're going to support that week. And we all seem happy enough. I mean, we, but I think it might be difficult if they woke up every day and thought, where's that? Because he missed breakfast because he either went to bed an hour before they woke up. But on the other side, I get to spend a lot more time with them in the afternoons and I probably attend more or I'm blessed enough to attend more than most people get to attend because during normal people who work nine till five don't often get to go to school and watch the kids do whatever they do, whereas I can. Right. And that is something Bond alluded to in the interview I did with him and Kevin last week as well, which takes me to Kevin. Kevin, I alluded in my intro to what you said you wanted how Bertology to bring. So why don't you give me an idea? What is your vision for what how Bertology is bringing to its members? Well, the thing is, is that I, you know, when you, when you realize for us, for Bond and Kevin, what how Bertology and Gary Halbert, in a larger sense, brought was goodwill. I'm amazed at how even when he's passed away and people didn't even discover who he was until he passed away, how much there's goodwill towards my dad. It's just like he's still changing people. He's still making people fall in love with him that, you know, he's never met. They just read the Bond letters, the Gary Halbert letters, and there's so much goodwill. And I think that they realized that Gary Halbert struggled and he had a lot of, you know, trial and error to get to success. And he was very, but when he got to that success, he didn't go, you know, I'm going to keep it to myself because I paid my dues and I'm not going to share with you the secrets to success. He just shared it with people. And I think that when people do that, when they, when they use this advice and they go and they get successful, they feel the same way. They don't, they feel a lot of appreciation and they really want to do this thing about giving back. And when we started this program, we said, well, let's see, you know, who can we ask to come in and help us do this? And when we call people, Bond talks about how, when he calls, he can't even get the invite sentence done before they're, they're saying, yes, I want to be a part of this. And so, and I do the same thing. I've called other people and they get really excited. And that's very different from saying, Hey, I'd like you to come help me be on this webinar or something like that. They really are like, really excited. And I find that they really want to, they want to keep on par. They want to be among that, that, that level of, of excellence, of knowledge and expertise. And so they will share and they go to the extra mile to try. So it's, and so we got people that are, are so excited. And they're like, I get, I'll tell you the secret. I'll share you the secret. I'll tell you this. And so you see that a lot with all these people. And it was funny, because when we announced that we were doing this, we had, I remember one guy in particular, he's like, I've got this big secret. I'll share it with your whole audience. You know, I love Gary. I'm so thankful for everything. And I wasn't sure if the guy was for real or not. And we exchanged a couple emails back and forth. They said, you know, I got to see what your secret is so that I know you're for real. And then he sent it to me the other day. And I'm like, Oh my God, that's brilliant. And you know, this is something that's made, you know, him, him and his clients a lot of money. But that's, that's, you know, that's a common phone call for us. And so I'm really like the excitement of the goodwill that people have towards our my dad and what he's done and stuff like that. And I usually don't try to call on certain favors for people, just for me. And so some of these people, I get this chance to not just interrupt their hour and stuff. But if I'm going to do that and suck all this secret information out of people, then I want to make the most of it and have a whole, you know, a whole audience here enjoying it and just getting, you know, every last little thing that's that these guys who some of you don't know some of these people, you won't know who they are and their names are. And I think that's really a great thing because these guys are not normally trying to be out there as a famous person. They're out there doing it. And we're able to call and ask around and find out who the who the serious players are. And these, you know, fortunately, many times they know exactly who Gary Hubbard is. And so they'll drop things to come in and do things for us to help us, you know, really learn the things that they do that have put them a cut above everyone else and made them a leader and had a competitive edge over everyone else. And I don't think they necessarily would normally just have a stranger ask them, Hey, can you do this? And they would they would give as fully and completely and really want to have a passion for helping the people, not just a willingness, but a passion for doing it. And so that's what I want. I want with all these people. And, you know, we just kind of asked ourselves, what is, you know, what, how much can we bring to how many different areas can we help everybody? Because everyone's going to have different things that they need to learn about and know. And I think we've pretty much got a really broad umbrella of all the expertise of marketing and copywriting that could ever be asked to put together. Nobody has turned us down as far as I know yet. So that's just to tell you that we put the we didn't put together what we could put together. We put together what we wanted to put together. And so far it's turning out beautifully. Okay. Wow. Thank you. Yeah. Kevin was telling me earlier about how he wanted to suck the marrow out of every single expert that comes on. So all of you how brutality presenters and how brutality presenters, you know who you are and be prepared Kevin's coming to get you. So in the vein of those people that work behind the scenes that you never know, but our absolute aces at what they do, want to talk to David Franklin. And David is one of those guys he, you know, we're dragging him kicking his screaming into the limelight. But because like Kevin said, it's a Gary Albert thing. He's excited to be here as far as I know. What I want to know from you, David, you have spent an incredible career in advertising and copy. Why are you so obsessed with copywriting? Wow. You know, the power of words to move people to action. And I mean, you know, if you really think about it, they've moved as people, you know, nations to war. They brought about peace. I mean, words are what makes everything happen in the world that happens. And the ability to put together the right words to solve a person's problem to help them make their dreams come true. You know, a lot of times we think of copy is just we're out to sell some stuff. And I'm sure that's the main reason most people are going to be part of this is they want to learn how to sell some stuff and they will. But it's really, you know, it goes further than that. The ability to write great copy. Well, Gary Albert, you know, greatness, anything you want in life is just one sales letter away. And that's one of the things I learned from Gary Albert. And it's so true, because you can change the world. You can make anything happen with a great sales letter. And so that, you know, that ability to get the right message to the right market at the right time and really solve some people's problems, really make their dreams come true. And probably that the thing I love most, you know, I've written copy for 30 years now. And most of my career was spent in traditional brick and mortar advertising. I owned an advertising agency. We did TV commercials, radio commercials, magazines, newspaper ads, all the normal stuff. And if the client was happy, and business appeared to be pretty good, everybody is good. As a matter of fact, the client would always get bored with their advertising burst, even if it was working, just because it was almost more entertainment than than really ads. When I discovered direct response advertising, the competition of, you know, when you split run test ads are exciting, you know, getting in there and knowing that here's a control that's maybe run for years. And you're going to go try and beat it, or you're just going to go head to head. And you're going to put two messages out there to a list and see which one wins. That is probably the most exciting part about about being a copywriter is the winning. But it's not because you just have a competitive spirit and want to win. It's because you know that through testing and through this constant, you can keep making the message better and better and better. I mean, there's just no end until you know, it just becomes perfect. So there's that. There's the fun of going up against other copywriters competing. I'm sorry, I'm rambling, but I love copywriting. What can I tell you? And it is that amazing power of just moving the world. And, you know, Gary Halbert was the best at it of anyone I've ever known. His ability to persuade was off the charts. And he did it in a way that you didn't even feel you're being persuaded. You're being told a great yarn, you know, his fabulous story. And at the end of it, you were just ready to fill in an order or, you know, fill in the order card or send your check or whatever. Great. That's fantastic. Thank you, David. That brings me to a great question for Bond. You know, we all are working in this business in our entire slate of presenters for Habitology. You're going to find people that are making millions in every area of marketing. But the folks I have gathered here, one of the things that we all do to some degree is write copy. So Bond, tell me, what is the best single line of copy you have ever written? And what made it great? Okay. Well, one, I want to apologize if you hear the airplane firing up in my background. That's my computer. I got to get a, I guess one with a more heavy video card capability because it sounds, you know, it fires up more often than normal when I'm on Skype. I would say that the best line of copy I wrote is not, you see, there's a difference between my favorite and the best. Now, my favorite is because I've written some really salacious fun stuff that just, it gets, you know, great copy often it reaches into you and grabs your attention in a way that you cannot ignore it. You can't force it, you know, you've got to click on it to find out what it is. And so sometimes you, you know, you're going down the dark side of human nature or you're getting really funny and stuff like that. And so that often presents your favorite pieces to copy. Like you're like, oh, that was so witty and clever and funny and everybody loved it and things like that. But in reality, I think I said the best single one line of copy I ever wrote is probably a subject line. If you're usually writing a really, your best line in the world is probably almost always a headline or a subject line. And for me, there's actually two that stand out, but I'm going to tell you about the one because I think it, because it was proven to be the most effective, which was the Holy Trinity of copywriting books. Now, let me explain why that works because you can use it in your own marketing. First of all, it, it was written to our list of people who are copywriters and interested in copywriting. So it was a perfect target match. Okay. So instead of being really clever, I mean, the other subject line, I was going to tell you about everybody loves it and everybody clicks on it and everybody reads it, but that doesn't mean they're all buying. This is one that proved to make people buy. And the open rate on that was so high. It was 51% on an unscrubbed list with thousands and thousands of names built up over a long time. And so it proves that the line was effective in getting everybody to open it. And let me explain why and then what went in it and how it performed. The reason it worked is if you're in copywriting, there's a, there's a magic in you when you add numbers to things. So if I say that when I say the Holy Trinity, you know that there's three. And so you're wondering what are the top three copywriting books if you're into copywriting. And you're wondering if your top three match my top three. And you're wondering, you know, why I'm giving, when I say Holy Trinity, why I'm giving such reverence to this, to these three books and so forth. And the curiosity is just so powerful that people are dying to know. At least the people who are interested in copywriting. And again, this is a principle that you do in your marketing. I don't care about anybody else. Other subject lines get everybody to open the email. My mother-in-law, my friends and cousins and everybody else because of out of curiosity, but this gets the right people to open the email. And inside that email, what I did was I used a kind of a level of psychology, a lot of people, or not, I'd say psychology, psychology as much as it was a tactic that I was playing with that worked really well. And what I did was I took three books there. One was the boring letters, which is written by my father and then edited it. And I made amendments to it and had the commentary to explain how it was working in the modern world. And in there, I also put in two other books, which by the way, I'm going to recommend everybody buy and read. One is called Scientific Advertising. This is the, I like the boring letters, that's the one my dad wrote, which is a series of letters to me because it's a great outline for exactly what's inside powerful copywriting, what's inside powerful marketing. And it gives you a great outline of the business. Scientific advertising is like a field guide to the whole business of direct marketing. It's a very short book. You're going to love it. When you get these books, by the way, these books I'm talking about are books that should be read once a year because every single time, even seasoned marketers who have read the boring letters or Scientific Advertising 20 years, you know, 20 times, every time you pick it up for a year, you go and you focus in on, you hone in, it's filled with so much stuff that you hone in on one aspect and you start to go apply it to your marketing. And it's super effective because it's timeless. And it's really touching based on the psychology and emotional triggers of selling, which do not change over time. Okay, you're feeling to be a part of something doesn't change over time. What you want to be a part of changes over time. Okay, with society and everything. But anyway, in the third book that I had recommended there was Robert Collier Letterbook. And in this situation, what I did, what I was actually trying to do was get a whole bunch of people that were buying my, our book to also buy the other books as well. And there were free versions of these books out there. In fact, there are free versions of all three books floating around out there. But these were, you know, I mean, and, you know, why bother to waste, you know, hunt around for something that's already pretty inexpensive compared to what it provides. But the point was, there were options and everything, all three of those books made it in the top four spots of Amazon's Copywriting section for that day. They all did, which proved that this work now I've had higher open rights. I've had higher, you know, I've had, you know, bigger money making days, all of this other stuff. But that single one line of copy is what drove people into it and actually hit the right mark to message match, offered the right things and solution to the people that I was marketing to and expand and it was effective and it worked. Now the other that third level thing that I was trying to, that I alluded to earlier at the beginning was that one of the things that nobody knew I was trying to do is I was trying to marry these books so that when you go to Amazon and you pull up the scientific advertising or you pull up the Robber Collier Letter book, you see the boron letters right next to it saying people who bought this also bought that and it worked. So now when you look up these books, you see them, you see this book and my father, you know, my father's book now is married with these other legends of direct marketing who he himself recommended and would have loved to have been connected to. And so the reason it was my favorite little small thing is because, or line is because it drove people in there, it had, you know, had numbers and curiosity and all the things you need in a subject line to get people to really open it. It was congruent with the message. So it didn't trick you into opening and then say, hey, by the way, you know, meet my dog. And it drove people to get right in there and start, you know, and it gave them a solution they wanted and it was effective and it had an effect that lasted a lot longer than just the people buying that book that day because you can still, you could still log on to Amazon and see the effects of that email every time you want to do it. So it was because it worked. It wasn't the most salacious thing I've ever written. It wasn't the most, you know, the Todry or anything like that. But it was very, very effective. And so it's probably my favorite single line of copy. Long answer, but that's it. Wow, that was a great answer. So that's fantastic. Thank you so much. I want to go back to David. And I want to find out, David, you know, we know you're obsessed with coffee. We know Bond's obsessed with coffee. I think probably we all are to some degree. But if you weren't a copywriter in this life, what would you be? Oh my goodness. I don't know. I've never been anything else. You know, probably I started selling stuff face to face. I mean, I literally sold air for many years. Let me explain. I ran radio stations. And that's what we did. We sold air time. And you learn a lot about rejection when you're walking into small business owners who are trying to figure out how they're going to keep the lights on and try and talk. I mean, it's been a whole bunch of money to get more people in because they don't get it. But you know, it was all those years of sales. I mean, I even sold cars once, many, many years of a whole different lifetime. But until you've gone out and actually sold face to face, you just don't realize, I don't know if anybody should ever write copy that hasn't ever had a door slammed on their face. I don't think you're qualified. So I, you know, probably I'd still be selling stuff is what I'd be doing. But that said, I have a rock fetish. My wife tells me. And what that's about is I kind of landscaping, particularly building water features, waterfalls, is a hobby of mine and a passion of mine. And I mean, I will go I'll hike forever to see a waterfall. And particularly a really good man made one I've got to see because I got to see if they nailed it. So that's probably, you know, I might in my next life, I might go build waterfalls at big fancy wine res or something. And I like to play in the dirt, you know, and I spend so much time in front of a keyboard that that that just helps me use a different part of my brain, you know, where where it's just putting, you know, one Lego block on top of the other kind of so to speak. And it's a creative process. So who knows, you know, but really the reality is, is that the marriage of selling and writing copy are very close. And it was kind of interesting because when I was in radio, you know, we're just, I mean, let's face it, you got to sell the airtime. It's not like a roll of carpet sitting on a shelf at a carpet store. And if somebody doesn't buy it today, it'll be there tomorrow. And somebody may buy it tomorrow. Well, airtime on a radio station when when today's over, all that time we could have sold was gone. It's just it's just gone, right? It seems with television, a newspaper that comes out anything. And so the focus was always on selling that airtime. But my epiphany and really kind of when everything changed for me was, you know, when I said, you know, it's really not the airtime these people are buying. It's the idea. It's the big idea that is going to tell their story, going to target the right prospects with the right message and get them to do business with them. So so when I figured out that it was about the story, it was about the copy, you know, and not about selling the airtime. And we really focused on that. My entire staff, I mean, that that became our obsession was to produce great ads for people that actually moved people to take action and do business with them. And we started focusing on that. And all of a sudden, we went from trying to figure out how we're going to sell that airtime to not have an aided sell been sold out all the time. You know, it became harder to figure out where we're going to put ads, then how we're going to sell ads, because we're focused on the right stuff. And that was helping our clients to actually grow their businesses through the right message. And so and really, that's what sales is about. I don't I don't you know, like I said, probably still be pounding on doors. I would hope that if I was still pounding on doors after all these years, I might not be getting as many slammed as in my face as as I did back in those early days. But I tell you, it really when you selling face to face, probably I, you know, I haven't done it in years. As a matter of fact, you know how hard it was to get me on camera. This is probably the first time anybody's seen my face on camera. In many, many, many years, maybe ever. So, you know, getting back out there and pounding on doors would be interesting after all these years. But it's really the same thing. It's that ability to to sell. But it does bring me to a great segue, though, back to Mark, because one of the things that we've been getting a lot of comments about with regard to your video was how you were approaching things in a completely new way that no one had ever thought of before we are looking at business holistically. So based on that, what would you call your definition of success? First, I think we should address David in his cape. I heard he has a cape. Lori, did you tell Mark about my cape? I think success is different for everyone. Success for me is being content with who you are. And to give you something that happened recently with the kids, something came up with my kids. One of the kids asked, so how do you know which one or which kid is going to be the best or successful? So my answer was the one that's having the most fun. Because success, I think, is a way of building your own universe or building something around you that you can live within. And regardless of how much wealth or how little wealth you have, it doesn't create that. You create that yourself. So my definition of success is to be content and to feel good about myself and also to give back, to work with other people, try and help them. And I think that one of the things here that's interesting with Harvardology is no one who is involved here really is not really about the money. Everybody who joined up, who said yes, none of them are paid. I mean, they did it voluntarily. And it's an acknowledgement of who Gary Harbour was. This is, for me, it's like a tip of the hat to say he was one of my heroes. But also, I think, to try and give other people a chance for their idea of success, it would be nice to see someone who is starting out or who is struggling and who wants to try and get somewhere to give of your time, to try and help that person achieve that idea of success. I think there's a reward there that becomes part of your own success. So that's the years ago, I'll just digress a little bit. Years ago, I owned a magazine, a publishing company, and I used to interview wealthy people. And one of the questions was, what would you advise other people if they wanted to become successful? And the number one answer when we analyzed it all was how did you become successful was hard work. But another thing that was that they had to want it. In other words, people will come along and they'll say, yes, I want to be richer, I want to be wealthy, or I want to have the big house, or I want to have this lifestyle. And they have a vague idea of what they think they want, but they've really got to want it. So whatever that dream is, whatever that goal is, somewhere deep down inside that person, they got to want it. You can't put it inside them. It's not our job for that person to wake up every day and for us to fill their head with want, the starting point is they have to want it. And then we can help them as a guide, as a mentor with experience and knowledge to help them achieve that goal. So to answer your question for me, success is being happy with yourself. Thank you. Great. Now that takes us back. You touched on something that Kevin and I had talked about earlier about the different levels of education that Habritology is going to bring to people. And Kevin was very passionate about the differences, whether you're a beginner or someone who is more experienced or an expert. One of the things you guys all have to know out there in Habritology land is every person I'm talking to right now and myself, we cannot wait to get in there and be taking the notes and learning everything. I mean, when you've got the people that we have got lined up, this is like we want to pick their brains. We can't wait to. Hopefully they want to pick ours a little too, but we really want to pick theirs. So Kevin, could you speak a little bit to what you were talking about with regard to various levels of education and understanding? Yeah, I think that it's interesting. When we did a survey to our list, we discovered that there's still a lot of people that are beginning. They don't know where to start. They don't know what comes first. Or they get excited about something and they get a kind of a path they have in their head as to how they'll do something. And so we encounter a few things that, you know, like SEO would be a great example of a kind of a Gordian knot of trying to figure out all the different ways that you can do all this stuff. And you'll find that everyone's talking over here. They're talking about doing this. They're talking about doing that. It could be PPC. It could be any of these other traffic sources or ways to skin a cat. And you find out that there's just so much on the menu that you don't even know which way to go. Or it just looks like it's insurmountable that there's just so many factors that you can't even begin to think about it. And what I want from the experts is this thing where we, excuse me, where we skip all of the chaos and everything and distill it down to just the simple things. Because I think one of the things that's most important is that, you know, I've learned how you can save a ton of time by if you're going in a wrong direction and someone's saying that doesn't work. We tried that. And that's just to waste the time. And just that one sentence from any expert that really knows what they're doing slashes a whole like hard knocks, had to learn it on my own thing. And you get ahead just that much faster knowing one major avenue you're going to spend a lot of time on, on not doing. The other thing is, is that I think that the way to get to master any of these areas that these experts know about is to first simplify. What's the most simple thing? You can get more complicated once you get the base down. But it's finding out this, don't focus on this, focus on this. These are the things that are most important that you should, you should focus on. And these are the things that really matter and count. And in addition to that, is that some of these guys are going to reveal advanced stuff. And so that's going to help people that kind of already have, like, oh, yeah, yeah, I know that part. You know, I know I've been doing this for a while. But they, but these are the experts of the experts. So they're going to know some advanced stuff. And they're going to tell you what's kind of emerging now or what they've discovered recently, and stuff like that. And so, but I think even me as a beginner, when I hear someone talking about something I know nothing about, say, like YouTube SEO or something like that, that might be of interest to me, or, you know, that might be a way someone's making a lot of money. And they'll talk about these things that are way, way out of my understanding, because I've never even started to get into that realm. And but I know that I want to store that piece of thing, they go, Oh, this blah, blah, blah, and I've never heard that technique or term or whatever. And I save that for when I do get there. And I, it also helps me if I talk to another expert and go, I heard this, what do you think of this is this, you know, and get to compare notes. So I think that you've got the advanced people that are going to get some, some tips from some, the really advanced people, but the beginners should also take notes and keep that stuff because they're going to get there at some point. And they're going to want to have that information ready for them. But the beginners, it's a lot about this, you know, 90% of this, don't focus on that, don't focus on this, this is what you need to focus on. And these are the things that are the biggest mistakes people make. And this is what will get you started in a head faster. And, and just that's, that's what I want. If we go into any of these categories, well, I don't know something, because there's going to be some, that's what I want to know, I want to know, where do I, where do I put my energies? What do I not waste my time on? What is the simple pass that I take? And, you know, even where are the sources? Where do you go for, for the next step and stuff like that? And then, then I just have what they, you know, they say that the XYZ widget up there, when you, when you get to this advanced stuff, you know, is going to be really cool. And I'm like, all right, I'm keeping that, I'm keeping that in my memory for when I get there. Great. So Bond, I want to come back to you. And, you know, Kevin just brought it up. There are things that everybody focuses on doing because they're either really good at them or because they really like doing them. And hopefully, if you're following Mark's map for success, it's both, that you're good at it and you like it. But what's the thing you hate doing? You can hear me, right? Yes, we can. Okay. I would say the number one thing that I, I mean, that sticks out in my mind, obviously, like when people are quick, what do you hate? I think the answer is commuting. When it comes to, you know, the one, but the one thing when, you know, this is what I want to explain about in related to marketing. I really don't, I live in Los Angeles, so commuting here really sucks compared to almost anywhere in the world. And I actually live surrounded by three of the busiest highways in the, or freeways in the entire world too. And so I get to like Mark, I get to design my life the way I want, when I want to work and so forth. So you won't catch me out there driving during rush hour at all. I basically design my life around that. But that's the one thing I really like about marketing. What you're going to see with Albertology is a lot of people think there is this one magic blueprint. And when you come to it, what you do is you find out that, you know, there's a way that you can get some more attention or get some branding done via social media or Facebook advertising, or there could be somebody who's, you know, really loves direct mail or really loves space advertising. And the truth is you could promote a business with a lot of these different avenues. And what I do like about marketing is there is now so many different ways to skin that cat, as they say, and that you get to choose on which ones you do and which ones you do not like. So if you feel like, you know, I'm not one of these people who wants to share my personal life or my, you know, any pictures of what I'm doing and stuff, you don't have to be on social media to make a business run. There are other people who don't want to interact with people. But large, one of the big secrets in marketing is to understand at what point people want to interact with you. They do. But you also have to understand when they don't, you know, and I think, you know, like I've often said, you know, the magic behind those old free recorded message campaigns was, you know, you felt safe knowing you're going to call up and get a recorded message. You didn't have to talk to anybody because you were unsure. Now, eventually, you know, you get to the point where you're so positive, hey, I want to call Bond because I want to pick his brain and I would love to get him on the phone. But you know, so the great thing about marketing is you really don't have to do anything to do something that you don't like. If you don't want to publish an Amazon book, you know, for authority, or you don't want to, you know, you don't want to run PPC campaigns, you don't have to. Now, you do have to do something. I'm not going to pretend that you can sit back and just think your way to success. You do have to, you know, you have to get up in the morning or the evening or whatever you do. And you have to be able to get things to get some stuff done and have a plan of action and go forward and, you know, make some mistakes and but just go forward and do things. But you really don't have to put yourself on the spot. You know, I'm I've given a few speeches in my time and I'm actually been told by a lot of people and I believe that I'm very good at it. But it makes me it makes me very nerve wracked. And from the moment that you say I'm going to go and give this speech up on stage to, you know, a few hundred people to the moment I actually am on stage. Now, five minutes after I'm up there, I'm having a good time. You know, I'm just being my own bucket mouth self. But up until that point, I get a little anxious. And that's because I really care about what the audience is going to hear and learn. I really do not want you know, everybody's now talking about it with my daughter and she says, Well, you know, if I'm just talking, I have no problem. But if I have these notes or a script to go by, I'm like, I like notes and scripts, because I'm guaranteed that I'm passing on something I know that's going to be a value to them. And I'm not going to lose my place. But the, but the thing is, I, you know, you find that I'm not out there a lot. And I get invited to speak at a lot of events and that I that I turn them down. And it's not just, you know, I mean, you know, sometimes it's scheduling or whatever you, but for the for a large part, I don't have to do the things, the parts of advertising and marketing that I don't want to do. And I can put more energy and focus into the other stuff. And this kind of goes back to what Mark was saying. The more you're having fun, your brain relaxes, and you have a good time, and you can start thinking of other things, you know, the minute the stress leaves you, and of a situation, we've all been there with something stressful, stressing you out, you're in there, your, your mind is going, I've got so much anxiety, what do I choose and everything like that. And the minute that stress is gone for whatever reason, the situation has changed or whatever, you go, I know exactly what I should have done, I could have done this, and I could have done that and so forth. That's because your brain was relaxed, because your brain was having fun. And if you're doing the part, if you're focusing on the parts that are having fun, if you are David and you're writing, okay, these are three great, you know, headlines I want to test, and you're having fun, you're going to write better headlines. If you are one of these people that thinks that, you know, social media, I really enjoy that part. And so what you, and you really, you're, you're hungry for clicks and likes and stuff like that, and you start to play around with it. You know, I myself started looking at social media, and I started playing with humor, and I, and I quickly, and I taught myself about how to make that punchline at the end. And I've measured the success based on how many likes I would get, you know, so I'll go into somebody's thread and their comment, and I want to make a comment, I'll come back and check, and I want to be the, I want to be the most liked comment in that thread. But the point is, whenever you're doing that you're having fun with, you're going to do better. And there's no longer one avenue to do everything, you know, it used to be, if you had a pizza parlor in your town, the only real way to advertise was either a billboard or the newspaper, you know. Now that, you know, that is really just not the case. There's a, there's a bunch of different ways you could do things in the buff, a bunch of different ways you can engage people. So if you're, don't want to do, you know, if you, if you really scared of face to face selling, you know, face to face selling is going, you know, David, right? You're going to, you can be, I mean, that's the best copywriters in the world usually came from places where they did a lot of face to face selling. But if you don't want to do that, you don't have to do that. You know, you could do it all via remote control. And that's a lot of, that's what my dad loved about direct marketing is he considered it persuasion via remote control. You know, he could send out letters out there, hit people to do things. And, you know, while he was at a movie. And so I think that I think the great part about marketing nowadays, especially, you know, because I grew up in the industry is every year it gets easier and there are more channels and stuff. And, but this is also comes with a warning. And I want us that'll have that, you know, should make your everybody's lives easier. Don't shoot for trying to do it all. You can't. I mean, you know, I mean, just, you know, just look at social media alone. Don't even look at advert, you know, space advertising, direct mail, remnant radio, anything else, just social media, you'd have to cover your, your image, your account, you'd have to cover your Instagram, your Snapchat, your Facebook, your Google plus and all these other things. And I know that there are programs out there like Hootsuite that'll help you kind of upload to all of them at once and stuff. But monitoring all that, you know, I mean, if you're really into it, and it's fun, you can, you can do that. And I've seen people change, you know, turn that into a money to a money making opportunity for them quite easily. But again, it's just, there's just no way that you can do all of it at once. And so don't bother to try just because somebody says, you know, you can publish an Amazon book or they teach you secrets, I'll teach you secrets about it. Doesn't mean that you have to do that or you should do that. What you should do is focus on the things that are effective, the things that give you leverage. And most importantly, back to what Mark's saying, the stuff that sounds like the most fun to start experimenting and playing with. Because if you having fun experimenting and playing with it, you will keep it up. It's the same thing. I mean, it's the same thing in life in everything. You go to ride, ride a bike, as soon as you have a little bit of fun doing it, all of a sudden, now you're expanding and you want to go faster and you're going to go jump the curb and you're going to take a little jump off, you know, and so forth. So, you know, don't try and do everything and just remember that you don't have to. And then in fact, I don't know anyone who does. I don't know a single guru that is well spread out across all, you know, the books, they're in their book stores, they have an infomercial going, they're running space ads, they've got a direct mail campaign, social media, peeper click advertising, and everything all going at once. I know companies that try and do that with huge teams of people. But I don't know a single, I don't know a single person that that's part of their daily routine. So, don't, you know, enjoy yourself, have fun, you know, pick on something, pick something that looks like it's going to be fun to play with. And, you know, don't worry about all the rest. It all comes in time. Fantastic. So, borrowing bonds analogy, let's take the training wheels off of this. And I want to put one question to all of you. We have about six, seven minutes left. So, let's, let me put this one question to all of you. Let's just make it a nice round table discussion. What is the one thing that you hope that somebody asks you during hermetology? What's the one thing you want to make sure that everybody gets to learn from you? Just feel free to jump in guys. Well, I'm, I'm going to answer it in a slightly different way. I'm going to say the one question I want to hear most people ask. And I want to hear most people ask, what should I experiment with next? And the reason for that is it's very good for Kevin and I are very good at being able to figure out, okay, this is a better leverage point for you. So, if you give us an option and say, what should I try next or what should I delve into and here are the three things I'm thinking of, we'll be very good at telling you, okay, we think this is the one that you should try first. But the reason I want to hear that question asked is I want people to understand that success and direct marketing is a process. It is not a winner fail situation. It's not a winner lose. It is not a succeed or fail situation. It is a process. If you get up to learn how to ride a bike, everybody who's ridden a bike fell down the first time they tried to ride it. Okay, maybe, maybe I wasn't looking and Kevin was the first guy to start doing wheelies as soon as he hopped on that seat. But I've never seen anybody actually do that. And it doesn't mean massive failure. We're not talking about falling down and breaking your leg while you tried to learn a bike, you know, learn how to ride a bike. You're actually, it's the analogy is simple. You, everything that you do, you can't be, you know, nobody set the Hemingway didn't set down, sit down and just start writing masterpiece works, works of art. I mean, excuse me, masterpieces of literature. And when you understand that it is a process and you understand that it is experimentation and you have fun with it, one, there's no limit to where you can go with that. That's, that's one thing that I like about it. But two, when you relax and start, you know, you having fun with it and you start experimenting with it, your success opportunity is so much greater. I see a lot of people making a couple of mistakes in marketing at the beginning that really kind of cements their future. They get locked into a plan. And when they get locked into the plan, they don't want to release it until it's perfect and everything's great. And so I want this, I want the, you know, I've got the headline, I did the math, I wrote a full page ad, I'm going to spend $70,000. I'm going to run this as a full page, front page, excuse me, full front page in one of the sections of the Los Angeles time. And they do all of this. And then if the phone starts ringing, they're ready to, you know, to go start buying Ferraris. And if the phone doesn't start ringing, they're, you know, want to pull the hoodie over their head and shrink away. The big marketing corporations who have it down, the people who's bread and butter and, you know, putting, you know, with, you know, this is the business that puts food on the plate for their family, on the dinner table for their family. They're people, what they do is they test three different hooks, you know, before they decide which ones to go with. They, they don't just write a piece of copy and go, Oh, this is a masterpiece. And I can't wait. I hope Kevin loves it. What they do is they go, Okay, well, we go this angle, this angle, this angle. And then they don't try, they just experiment with it. And they, they go, they hop in there. And the audience tells them, you know, this one worked. And then you reverse engineer it, you say, Okay, this one really worked. And what do we think are the reasons that they did it? And this goes back to even the example I gave you earlier. I had the idea of trying to marry the Boron gutters book to another book. I did it. I did it successfully. And then I wanted to find out if it really worked. So I had to recreate it. So I reversed engineered what I did and replicated, and it worked and it worked like gangbusters. So the point that I'm saying is if you understand that this is a process that you're going to be experimenting around in the lab a little bit. And, you know, and this carries on to you, it's your attitude. So when you're a loved ones, now I grew up in an entrepreneurial family. So I've had a lot of support. But I know a lot of people breaking out there don't have the full support of their family loved ones who don't understand why they're giving up a job or why they're trying to do something in the entrepreneurially started business. And I've always had that benefit. But even without that benefit, if you tell people, Well, I've got these three ideas, I'm going to test out, see which ones people like, and then I'm going to work on the one that comes back. You know, you take all that pressure off of you, you start stop, you start having fun, you start enjoying the commentary and the feedback you're getting, you're going to find even new stuff to venture off and to go do, you're going to, they're going to come out with, you know, Hey, this is what resonated with me when you said that, you say, Oh, yeah, I know what you mean. And they give you a comment and you're like, that comment wasn't what I meant, but it's what it came through. And you're writing it down and you're saying, Okay, this is something that, you know, this is going to be incorporated into my new copy, you know. So the question I want to hear everybody ask is what should I experiment with next? It shows that you're ready to have a little fun. It shows that you're planning this out as a process. And you're not, you know, I found this one solution to my, to a problem I had. And I think there's millions of people who also want to pay for the solution. So I'm going to build this big campaign and release it at once. Now that's not the way that it should be done. Okay, what I open this up to everybody, gentlemen, Mark, David, Kevin, what's the one thing that you hope someone asks? Because realize bond just spoke there. So what do you want to hear someone ask you? What is your, what's the thing you're hoping brings into discussion? Christine, I think the number one thing I like to hear people ask me is, you know, may I buy you a beer? But, you know, seriousness, though, you know, really, the question I had when I dove into this mysterious world of online and internet marketing and all these things was, where do I start? And, you know, bond just hit on some great things. You know, this is a process. I can't tell you how many people I've said, you know, I'm just not that creative person. I mean, how do you learn to be creative so you can write great copy and all this and it's really not about creativity. I mean, it's not anything I was born with. It's not anything anyone's born with. It's a process as bond was just saying, where you figure out what's that next step? What's that next thing you need to learn? What's the missing pieces to your puzzle? And I think it's important to really understand what your puzzle looks like, too. What is it you want out of this? That's probably the question everybody going into this probably really should ask themselves. You should know, what is it I'm really hoping to gain from this? I mean, what are the areas that I feel I'm, you know, not as strong as I could be? What do I think are missings? And I remember for me in the beginning, it was always, well, at any point, it was kind of like, where do I start? I mean, people were throwing out kind of big picture ideas and philosophies. But it, you know, I wanted an instruction manual, you know, start here, you know, you go by a desk and it comes in a box. And it's step one. And I didn't use to ever follow those. And so I'd end up having to take up all back apart and put it back together. But I learned over the years that those instructions are actually there for a reason. And really, that's it, you know, you got to start somewhere, you got to start at the beginning. And it doesn't matter whether it's it's about writing the copy, or finding the market determining if there is a market for your product, whatever it may be, there's, there's a series of steps. And they're not hard. They're more a matter of doing, they're more a matter of getting in action, and just saying, okay, knock out 123. So I hope the things that people will ask, and I don't think it's one thing. But maybe it is and isn't, because it'll be a whole bunch of different things. But I think the one thing I hope people ask is, you know, here's where here's the roadblock in front of me. You know, I get to this point, and I just feel like I hit a wall, and I don't know where to go next. So where do I go next, you know, and like I said, for a lot of people, it's going to be how do I start, where do I start? Because that's a tough one. And then they're going to hit road bumps, or road blocks, speed bumps, whatever you want to call them along the way, they're going to, they're going to stop them. And sometimes these things will stop people dead in their tracks. I mean, that's it, game over. And that's really sad, because, you know, people, people need to raise the bar higher for themselves, because you know what, you keep doing that, and you're going to find you can reach it. And you're going to reach it more often and more times than you even thought possible. But to do that, you've got to, you can't let things stop you. And often what stops us is our unwillingness to ask somebody that's been where we want to go, how to get there. Or, you know, we have to, we have to kind of ask the embarrassing questions, the ones that might make us look stupid for asking. And I hope people will do this, because we've all been there. I mean, the people, you're going to hear from people who have had the benefit of so much experience of actually doing this stuff, spending the money, beating their head against the wall, going, crap, I got to try something else, because that didn't work. And you're going to have the benefit of all this experience that they can tell you and teach you and show you what they've learned. But you've got to be willing to ask the question and not be embarrassed about it, because I don't think there's ever been anything like this where you're going to have that ability to get in the room with these people, raise your hand, and say, Hey, I got a question. And you ask this, and you're maybe even a little afraid to ask it. And you hear this big sigh of relief in the room, because everybody else went, Oh, thank God, I was wondering that. And now I don't have to ask that question. I look stupid because they did. So ask the questions that you really want answered, because there's going to be the greatest group of people I've ever seen assembled. I mean, I am thrilled to learn from them. And that's what they're here for. They're here to take your tough questions and give you their answers, but even beyond that help you kind of work through it and solve it and move on. And I think we're all going to take that from this thing. You know, this is one of those things where, you know, there's a saying where, and I'll get it wrong. But basically, if there's, you know, two people together, you have, or whatever, you have one person, you have one mind, you put two people together, you have three, because you have this person's mind, you have this person's mind, and then you together in this collaboration, you create a third one that is the combination of the two, you know. And in this group, we're going to have that expanded way beyond that, because you're going to have all these people with all of this great knowledge. And as it goes on, it's going to just evolve. And, you know, there's going to be amazing discoveries, not just in how to do things, but in where it is you're trying to go, what it is you're trying to do, why you're doing it. I mean, this is going to be a journey of discovery for everybody involved, and an exciting one. Okay. So, Mark and Kevin, anything you'd like to add to the what is the question you hope somebody asks? Okay, what about you, Mark? What do you hope that someone asks you? I think a more interesting question would be what they would ask themselves, rather than what they would ask me. That's probably the one. If I was sitting on the other end of this screen right now, I'd be like, who is that woman, and why do I care? I'll tell you guys, I'm kind of like your Greek chorus, okay? I am a writer. I have a bachelor in fine arts in creative writing and a minor in English literature. I have the formal education, and I'm fairly new to, but somewhat successful so far, at copywriting. And I absolutely love it. I have to admit, I've got that competitive spirit thing going on, you know, and when I get to split tests against other really good copy with people who have 20, 30 years in experience, and even if I just like get close, let alone if I win, man, it's like, I can take that all the way to the bank. I may not make a ton of money on that split test, but boy, I feel great when I go to bed at night. It's the kind of instantaneous feedback on your writing that as a traditional writer in fiction or anything else, you don't get. You don't get that instantaneous feedback. And in this way, you get to see, did what I mean come across? You know, so that's fantastic. So when you ask yourself, is this something I can really do? Guys, I am a 50 year old woman who is starting on a brand new career path in life. I'm a single parent, and I'm doing this for real. I just was in LA last week interviewing Bond and Kevin. I'm doing this, and I write copy every single day. I split test every single day except the one day we don't win splits. And I can do this. So if I can do this, I don't see why anyone else couldn't sit down in front of their computer at home and do it too.
